speaker: interviewer
text: It's the 7th of March, 1995. We are interviewing Anna Reich. The interviewer is Vanessa Ring. We are in Sydney, Australia, and the language is English.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't have to be filmed before now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, my name is Vanessa Ring, V-A-N-E-double S-A R-I-N-G. And it's the 7th of March, 1995. I'm interviewing Anna Reich, and it's Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling. Camera is ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you begin by telling me your name and how to spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I am Anna, A-double N-A, Reich, R-E-I-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lipschutz, L-I-P-S-C-H-U umlaut-T-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 14 of the 9, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that makes you--?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in Krakow, in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, we'll begin with your life before the war. If we could start first with your family, did you have any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have one sister. And my mother and my two aunt, we did live together. And life wasn't-- at-- at time, it was better. At times, it was not very good. I did go-- I was a child when it was depression. And from this time, I have not very nice memories. It was a shortage on food. And in the winter, cold winter in Poland, there was some shortage of fuel. And-- but my mother was always very eager-- we was-- to educate us well. And I was a good student. So they-- all my accents was always good. And so I went to primary school and later, to a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] commerce and-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] economic and commerce school in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we could just talk about your family and your home life, could you describe your home for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: We did live in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two-- three rooms with my mother and my two aunts. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was not luxurious, but comfortable in a way, as the time was such that it's hard to compare with today's comfort. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of area did you live in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived in a suburb of Krakow called Podgorze.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of an area was that? Was it an assimilated area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was-- Krakow was an area where the-- a lot of Jewish people did congregate and felt more comfortable. Also, traditionally, they did congregate in a part of Krakow called Kazimierz. But we did live in Podgorze, where also quite a lot of Jewish people did live, which just dispersed outside-- what I did think it was [LAUGHS] ghetto, voluntary ghetto. In the olden time, actually, it wasn't a voluntary ghetto. The Jewish people was-- it was a ghetto then. But of course, later, the people was free to disperse to live wherever they did want, yes. I did have a-- I did have a lot of Jewish and non-Jewish friends. I had a very good non-Jewish friend, Polish girl. And my family was traditionally Jewish, but not terribly orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you follow the different rituals?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did have a Jewish education to some extent. And my mother did always care about the education. And definitely, we identify ourselves as Jewish, observed all the major holidays, and-- but not orthodox. Traditional.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to synagogue three times in a year. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that an actual synagogue in the area?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were some synagogues. But for the-- the High Holy Days, there was not enough accommodation, because most of the Jews, or all of them, were traditionally observing the High Holy Days. So sometime, some people which did have a bigger-- big accommodation did let their places. And I was-- they was usually called [? 'shtiebel.' ?] And what was it-- they was transformed for was usually two rooms-- one for men, one for women. And that's where they pray and observe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what kinds of festivals or rituals?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was Rosh Hoshanah, and Day of the Atonement, Yom Kippur, and sometime for Passover.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do any of those times particularly stand out in your mind, that you could describe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was always very keen [LAUGHS] when my mother went to synagogue, that she looks nice. [LAUGHS] And I remember taking big pride of making her look nice. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a happy time. And we always got new clothes for such occasion. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] yes, I associate this time with a happy time in my life, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a painter.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of painting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not artist painter-- he was a commercial painter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Painting houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Houses, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did he work for? Poles and Jews, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he worked for himself.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind-- the people whose houses that he painted, were they mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he would take jobs whatever, you know, not just-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a there was a feeling of-- anti-- anti-Semitic feeling towards the Jews. But in my town, not violent. But I remember-- actually, I remember as little girl, they did like me. And I congregated with the non-Jewish children, and I played with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember on one occasion, they was cross with me for some sort of reason. And they called me, 'Jewish girl, Jewish girl.' And that was a sort of insult [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I suppose to make me unhappy about. [? Alright?] ? And did it make you unhappy?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't like the comment. It didn't make me unhappy, but I hate them for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was seven.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that kind of comment come out from time to time over the years? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I myself-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I myself did not find so much-- I didn't [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] experience on-- on myself too much of the anti-Semitic things. I do remember the landlord was not Jewish with the house, and [LAUGHS] there wasn't such a comfort. And a lot of the places, the homes did not have their own bathrooms or toilets. There was a toilet which quite a few people did have to go. And in the winter, it was very uncomfortable, because it was outside the-- And when something went wrong with the system, he always was very angry and cold. [LAUGHS] 'Well,' he said, 'Jews [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] are still used to-- not used to the comfort. They should--' it's very hard to translate from Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: He did think that the Jews should still use the toilet behind the house, [LAUGHS] you know, sort of thing. And whenever something went wrong, he did use these terms, right? But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] otherwise, I don't think I did experience of too much anti-Semitic. What about in the schools?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. I did-- when I was a little girl, I remember one teacher in particular. She was in infant school, and her son was a priest. And she loved me very much. I was her favorite. [LAUGHS] So those are glimpses which I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when when people made these anti-Semitic comments, did you feel-- did you wonder why they were saying them?

speaker: interviewee
text: If anything, it did make me more united with the people. And it did make me realize that it's something-- I always did feel very deeply that something should be done, that we have a homeland. And that we are not just guests to be insulted whenever they wish. And therefore, I did join a Zionist movement as a young girl called [? Akiva. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a great atmosphere and wonderful feeling for looking into the future. And I did love all the songs which they did sing, because they was full of hope, where the Yiddish songs were terribly sentimental and beautiful, but very sad. And that did make me very happy to join this movement. And I remember on many occasions-- [SOBBING] [? Terrible. ?] Crying with help that there is hope for Israel. And we did have those beautiful songs, which I still remember, which were full of hope that people would go to Israel and-- [SOBBING] Stupid. And that Israel would eventuate. They were terribly dedicated people which did-- it was some boys which were from very well-to-do homes and girls. And they were so dedicated to this purpose. They did strip themselves of all the comfort. And [SIGHS] they were the 'haluzim,' which only did sing of one thing, to go to Israel and do hard work. There was a lot of places which was trying-- it's called 'hachsharah.' They was-- the people was trying to be prepared for a hard life. And they really was fantastically dedicated people. I was very--

speaker: interviewer
text: So hachsharah was a preparation?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was very young then. [LAUGHS] And on one occasion, I remember it was a very rainy day. And because they was so rough, and you know, they-- and I was still sort of a little city girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came in gloves, and it was raining-- I did have umbrella. And they all-- they all laughed, because they said, 'Don't be so silly. You don't need all this,' you know? And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, then, I went to school. And it was-- I interrupt-- I didn't carry on with this movement for like some of those people. Because, you see, they-- the parents [SIGHS] of the girls did not want them to harden up so much, and go to this hachsharah, which was a big hardship, actually. It was a hardship. They did have to work in temperature below zero, in doing all different tasks. And-- but it was a great spirit. They always congregated later together in the evening, and they'd sing. And it was really fantastic.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did your parents ask you not to do it, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't. I just-- my life did go the way that I went to school, and-- to high school, and to-- I was very absorbed in my study. And I always cherished the movement, but I didn't carry on.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then what did you do in your leisure time, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a family involvement, great family involvement. It was-- we were in very, very big family. My mother did have six sisters, and there was so many cousins, and you know. So it was always a lot going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your families, your-- your parents' families live in the same area as you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not all of them. Not in the same-- no. And my grandmother did live in a different part again called [? Ludvinov. ?] My cousins was some living in the country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- but we did live close to each other, so we visited each other very often.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then as you grew older, you went to college. It was an economics college?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that was like secondary and tertiary sort of education. You could go to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] university to further your study in economics. And it was a very good education. It was indeed in-- I consider myself to have a good education. That I was not a fool in-- in many ways, you know. It was quite complete, the education, like you could recognize what materials, different texture, or is this wool, or certain metals. And you know, you haven't-- you didn't have an education in the depth of every part of, but you were very well prepared for, like, a general knowledge. And there was literature. There was physics. There was geography.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you also came out with actual skills at the end?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- [SIGHS] well, I would say I rather came out with a good general education. And yes, I could-- with this education, I could apply myself to all different sort of things, mostly in commerce and economics.

speaker: interviewer
text: There wasn't a school that was just for Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was co-educational. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was all very-- how you say it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Inter-denominational?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, that's-- that's the word, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you mixed with everybody there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but there were a lot of Jewish girls as well. Yes, it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever experience anti-Semitism there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not myself, I must say. There were some Hebrew schools as well, but I did not attend those. I did have some private education in Jewish religion. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It wasn't in such a great depth.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I-- no, I must say I can't recall it. Maybe some small incidents, but generally, I don't think I can say-- Well, there was a general feeling when the Holy-- High Holy Days was, and there was a lot of Jewish people. And there was this general feeling of not tolerating the way the Jew did have the beard and looked different. And, you know, it was not the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] great tolerance in the air, you know what I mean? Did you feel different from the Hasidic Jews and--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't know. I somehow always inclined to blend. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't think that it is necessary to be dressed so separate. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I have-- as I say, mainly, I was guided by tradition and the love for the Holy Days. And the family, we did have Friday night every week. And for Shabbat, it was always special preparation. So we were traditional, but not orthodox, you know? And how did you feel about the orthodox people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were some very lovely people. But they were, in a way, a group for themselves, you know? It probably is today the same. [LAUGHS] And I have nothing against them.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there wasn't much mixing?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? I ?] [? guess ?] [? because, ?] you see, there was-- they were not such a tolerant-- I did sometimes feel maybe, why don't I reserve this for just special times? Or so it doesn't-- you know, it doesn't point the finger so much. That's how I felt, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then as the years went on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Gee, it's amazing how much I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, it's all coming out, yeah. As the years went on, and the war drew closer, what kinds of changes came about in people's attitudes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I did-- I did work-- first, can I tell you? I worked at one firm in Krakow which was a very special job. And.. those people which could get a job in this particular firm was lucky, because they was always special gratitude. The wages was better. And-- and the bosses was Jewish. The name was Alexandrowicz. They was very, very well known, very philanthropic family in Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a wholesale-- huge magazines [? right ?] through Poland of paper, paper in all sorts of ways-- bulk paper, newspaper. And it was-- it was a very big, profitable business. And I worked there. And that did bring me to the brink of the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The bosses did have some orchards in Israel, I remember. And my boss was so lovely. There were two brothers, Sigmund and-- oh dear, I can't seem to remember the other name. And everyday in the morning, when I opened my desk, there was a big Jaffa orange there for me, which was such a lovely feeling. And they went to Israel, what I understand, in the beginning of the war. They did-- I think they ran away, but I'm not sure what happened so that I couldn't follow it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I was working in the office, bookkeeping. Part-- it was-- the bookkeeping department was very, very big. There was-- I don't know, must be 30 people working in just this department. And I was one part of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you like a favorite? Is that why you got the Jaffa orange?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they was terribly generous people and terribly loving people. I did never ask [LAUGHS] or anybody else did find the orange in their desk. But I was always very happy to find one. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you mix with the other people who worked there and socialized with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish, everybody, or--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was all Jewish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you actually get the job?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was interesting, because to get this job, it was almost impossible, because everybody tried. But when I got my good result from school-- I [LAUGHS] remember I tried to look a bit more grown-up I was 17. And I remember putting on my auntie's outfit. And I did think, I'll just try to go there and ask for the owner to have a word with him. And when I come there, it wasn't easy, because they was-- they did have their offices, and it wasn't easy to speak to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But one of the-- office people came and asked me, 'Can I help you?' And I said, 'I would like to speak to Mr. Alexandrovicz. And he said, 'Well, what it is about?' And I said, 'Well, should I tell you I'm trying to get a job here?' And in this instance, I remember Mr. Alexandrovicz came out of the office. And this man who worked there-- what was his name? Can't remember. He laughed, and he laughed towards-- Mr. Alexandrovicz came closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, 'This is a young lady who tried to get a job here.' And he looked at me and smiled. And he said, 'Wait a minute,' and asked me to the office, in the office. And I said, 'Yes,' and that my wish always was to work here. And somehow, he was impressed, and I got the job.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHING] And then did you have much contact with him after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we did have a daily contact with the bosses, because they work-- they were almost at all the time in the office-- in the business, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you working there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it must be two years, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you start working there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was '38. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '38, yes. And then, when the war started and the bosses did run away-- actually, I must remember-- there were also people employed, not Jewish, actually. In the office, they was Jewish. But there was non-Jewish-- three brothers, I remember, Kafchinski was their name, lovely people who was very involved on the high position in this business, yes. But then, when both brothers Alexandrovicz fled, the business was taken by what was called then a 'Treuhander.' And I remember his name, a German-- Kiesel was his name. And well, the bosses wasn't there, of course. The business still did carry on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain what a treuhander is?

speaker: interviewee
text: treuhander was-- is a German [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] word for a person who steps in as an official to take over the business. And that was many, many Jewish firm [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was disowned, and then the German [? Treuhanders ?] did step in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you continued to work there after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I continued to work there until until ghetto was formed. I remember-- it was a very strong impression, because we did think that the Polish was well-equipped. As a young person, I did think their uniform was always spic-and-span and tidy. And I did think, somehow, Poland is strong. The Jews, I must also add-- the Jews was during the [? Marshal ?] Pilsudski reign-- The Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] I think that was-- I would like to put exactly when his reign was, but [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it's a very well-known fact that during his reign, Josef Pilsudski-- the Jews did have the best time in Krakow-- well, in Poland. And he was a very tolerant man. The Jews really did, at this time, enjoy freedom and prosper. And I remember when he die from-- the school took part in the funeral. And I was very moved by it. And this was a dying era for a lot of freedom for the Jews in Krakow.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I think it was 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: And things began to change after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the things did change after that, yes. And then, of course-- as I said, I did think Poland is strong. I was very patriotic, in fact, [LAUGHS] and very emotional. When they did sing the Polish national hymn, I always remember having tears in my eyes, you know? Somehow I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you identify as a Polish person or a Jewish person?

speaker: interviewee
text: Both, both. Right. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, I did think Poland is strong for some reason. And when the first German tanks [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] entered Krakow, and I did see it, that was, to me, like people from the other planet. It was so newly equipped, and so strong, and so overpowering. And so [SIGHS] then I understood, and I felt how weak Poland was. And the bad--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we might just leave it there.
